vision_report.compositional_truth_check: The registry is largely unsupported by visual evidence. P1 (String of Nickels) is absent. P2 (Mexican Mint) and P4 (Silver Guest) are not identifiable as distinct specimens. P3 (Pothos) is present, anchored by the white rabbit figurine.

inventory_reconciliation.P1: Systemic Loss: No plant matter visible in the yellow pot.
inventory_reconciliation.P2: Systemic Loss: No identifiable mint foliage present.
inventory_reconciliation.P3: Present: Pothos specimen identified by leaf structure near the white rabbit anchor.
inventory_reconciliation.P4: Systemic Loss: No identifiable silver guest foliage present.

plant_audit.P1_Yellow_Pot: Empty soil surface; no signs of life.
plant_audit.P2_Black_Pot: Appears to contain only soil and debris; no active growth.
plant_audit.P3_Black_Pot: Contains a Pothos specimen and a white rabbit figurine. The plant appears stable but lacks vigorous new growth.
plant_audit.P4_Shared_Black_Pot: Merged with P2; no distinct plant matter observed.

biome_observations.soil_texture: Appears consistently dark and damp across all pots.
biome_observations.debris: A metal spoon has been introduced as a new intervention in the yellow pot (P1) area in the current image.

temporal_deltas.Image_Earliest_to_T-2: Minimal change; pots remain largely dormant.
temporal_deltas.T-2_to_T-1: Lighting conditions shifted to a lower spectrum, obscuring detail.
temporal_deltas.T-1_to_Current: Introduction of a metal spoon into the yellow pot (P1) zone.

vision_report.visual_health_inference: The biome is in a state of severe neglect or dormancy. P3 is the only surviving specimen. The lack of foliage in P1, P2, and P4 suggests either total plant loss or extreme senescence.
anomalies: New Introduction: Metal spoon placed in the yellow pot (P1) in the current image.
Systemic Loss: P1, P2, and P4 appear devoid of viable plant life.
vision_report.narrative_description: The botanical environment is currently dominated by a single Pothos specimen (P3). The other pots (P1, P2, P4) are effectively empty of visible vegetation, indicating a systemic failure of the original biome registry. The recent introduction of a metal spoon into the yellow pot suggests human intervention or a change in maintenance tools, though no biological benefit is currently visible.
